content.method: Structured elite interviews and document analysis of maintainer public statements and legal filings; legal analysis of precedent (Curia ruling) and comparative constitutional analysis of religious-institution autonomy versus anti-discrimination obligations in similar EU jurisdictions.
content.question: Do church-maintained and minority self-government schools regard their admissions exemptions as a negotiable operational choice or a core institutional autonomy right, and under what legal or political conditions would they accept quota mandates or zoning constraints?
content.why_it_matters: This shapes the feasibility ceiling for T4 (zoning/exemption reform) and T5 (quota mandates): if maintainers treat exemptions as non-negotiable rights grounded in prior jurisprudence (e.g., the 2015 Curia ruling), legal reform attempts may fail or trigger prolonged litigation regardless of policy design quality.
